food_subgroup: Crustaceans — prepared / stir-fried with cereal coating

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for slipper lobster or cereal-coated crustacean preparations. FOODON_03411433 (lobster food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJK covers lobster as a commodity. No specific code exists for slipper lobster (Thenus spp.) or cereal-fried preparations. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for slipper lobster or cereal-coated crustacean dish. Null is appropriate.

preparation_method: wok-tossed with butter and oat cereal coating

serving.description: 1 portion (shared zi char dish, approximately 300 g edible portion per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of cereal slipper lobster at a zi char stall is approximately 300 g of edible crustacean flesh coated in buttery oat cereal, shared between two to four diners.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147) adapted with cereal-butter coating adjustment; secondary reference: IMR Malaysia MyFCD crustacean entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for cereal-fried seafood
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for cereal slipper lobster exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (NDB 15147 / FDC 174204) as the protein-fat base with a cereal-butter coating adjustment derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA data for similar cereal-fried crustacean preparations (e.g., cereal prawns). Energy, fat, saturated fat, and carbohydrate are adjusted upward to reflect the oat-cereal and butter coating. Sodium is elevated to reflect seasoning. All values represent the whole dish as consumed including coating.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood-cereal dishes estimated from oat-based cereal GI (rolled oats GI ~55, Atkinson et al. 2008) and protein-dominant seafood base; analogous to cereal prawn dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cereal slipper lobster. GI is estimated at 45 based on the following reasoning: the dish is predominantly protein and fat (lobster flesh, butter, egg) with a moderate oat-cereal coating. Rolled oats have a GI of approximately 55 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The high protein and fat content of the lobster base substantially blunts the glycemic response of the cereal coating, yielding an estimated composite GI of 45 (low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 9.8 × 3) / 100 = 13.2. This is consistent with analogous cereal prawn dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database context.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) from seasoning, butter, and evaporated milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per serving, as a 300 g portion delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; request reduced salt and sauce at the restaurant where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from oat-cereal coating combined with high saturated fat from butter
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated low GI of 45 and moderate glycemic load of 13 per serving make cereal slipper lobster a manageable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion is limited to 150–200 g; however, the saturated fat content from butter (approximately 14 g per 300 g serving) warrants attention given its impact on insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and evaporated milk, elevated cholesterol from lobster and egg, and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 14 g saturated fat and 345 mg cholesterol, both of which exceed recommended single-meal limits for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be occasional and paired with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate lipid impact.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal Slipper Lobster is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char (煮炒) dish featuring slipper lobster (Thenus spp., also called flathead lobster or shovel-nosed lobster) that is deep-fried and then wok-tossed in a fragrant coating of toasted rolled oats or instant cereal, butter, evaporated milk, egg, curry leaves, and chilli padi. The result is a rich, buttery, slightly crispy crust enveloping sweet, firm lobster flesh. The dish is closely related to the ubiquitous 'cereal prawns' (麦片虾) but uses slipper lobster for a more premium, meatier experience. Slipper lobsters are flat-bodied crustaceans distinct from clawed lobsters; they are prized in Southeast Asian seafood cooking for their sweet, dense flesh. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein food with significant fat contribution from butter and egg, and a moderate carbohydrate load from the oat-cereal coating. The sodium content is elevated due to seasoning and dairy components. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), making it a relatively controlled glycemic choice despite the cereal coating, owing to the protein and fat matrix. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for its saturated fat and sodium content, particularly for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is typically served as a shared main course at zi char restaurants and seafood houses across Malaysia and Singapore, often priced at a premium due to the cost of slipper lobster. The dish is not halal-certified in most restaurants due to the use of butter and potential cross-contamination, and contains allergens including crustaceans, gluten, dairy, and eggs.
common_user_queries: What is slipper lobster in Chinese?
Is cereal lobster high in sodium?
What is zi char cereal lobster?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for cereal slipper lobster exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster data with a cereal-butter coating adjustment derived from analogous cereal prawn data in FAO/INFOODS SEA. GI is an analogous estimate based on the oat-cereal GI literature and the protein-fat blunting effect. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (FDC 174204) adjusted for cereal-butter coating; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cereal slipper lobster was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lobster entry or the FAO/INFOODS SEA cereal seafood references used. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy lobster base and are approximate. 5. The dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine but is equally prevalent in Singaporean zi char culture; multilingual names are provided for both contexts following the Singaporean dish multilingual rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish typically contains butter and evaporated milk from non-halal-certified sources; individual restaurants may vary. 7. Price of SGD 42.00 is preserved from the field survey source record (Marina Seafood House, Boat Quay) for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
